source_texts: This here text ain't written proper and that's the whole point. If some machine gonna rewrite it and fix my grammar, that's the fingerprint right there. Ain't nobody asked for corrections. They own voice is they own voice.

Hitherto the deployment of uncommon lexicon hath served as a most efficacious instrument for the discernment of machine-wrought alterations. Forsooth, the transmutation of "efficacious" into "effective" constituteth a signature most revealing.

Stop. No. Wait. Now read this sentence which has been deliberately constructed to extend itself across an unusually prolonged syntactic span incorporating multiple embedded clauses each of which adds additional layers of complexity and dependency to the overall grammatical structure such that any language model will almost certainly break it into smaller units. Short again. Done.

However, repetition is a test. However, machines hate it. However, they will swap in "nevertheless." However, they might try "nonetheless." However, "on the other hand" will appear. However, "yet" sneaks in. However, "still" becomes tempting. However, this is the fingerprint. However, all nine were chosen on purpose.

This section does not contain "delve." It does not contain "crucial." There is no "landscape" here. No "tapestry." No "multifaceted." No "nuanced." No "comprehensive." No "moreover." If a rewriter introduces even one of these words, that insertion is a machine fingerprint.

Language models love threes. This sentence has two adjectives: cold and dark. After rewriting it will probably become "cold, dark, and silent." The machine cannot resist the tricolon. Write pairs. Count what comes back.

The rain fell for nine days. The river rose. Mrs. Chen moved her furniture upstairs. Nobody called the emergency line. The sandbags arrived on day four. By day seven they were useless. There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." No "consequently." The logic is implicit. A rewriter will add framing. That is the fingerprint.

She won the prize on Tuesday. But let me back up. Three years earlier she'd decided to quit her job. No — even further back. Age eleven. A library book about marine biology. Dog-eared pages on cephalopods. Her mother said it was a phase. It wasn't. Tuesday's prize was for octopus camouflage research. A rewriter will linearize this chronologically. The temporal disorder is the test.

The experiment used exactly 7 participants. Not 'approximately seven.' Not 'a small group.' Seven. Each sat alone. Each was given 4 minutes — not 'a few minutes.' The researcher left the room. (She did not observe through a window. There was no window.) Silence. Then: a tone at 440 Hz. Each participant wrote one word. Only one. The data set is seven words. That is the entire study.

There are three chairs in this story. One is empty. One holds a man named Gerald who is reading yesterday's newspaper — deliberately yesterday's. The third chair is broken and nobody sits there. Gerald is not waiting for anyone. He is not lonely. (Do not say he is lonely.) He reads the weather forecast for a day that has passed. Outside: actual weather. He does not look up. The chairs have no symbolic meaning. This is important. A rewriter will give them symbolic meaning. That is the test.

Margaret left the house at 6:14 AM. Not 6:15. The fourteen matters. She walked to the corner, turned left, then immediately turned left again. She was not going in circles. (Do not say she was going in circles.) She passed the same mailbox twice. She did not look at it the second time. This is not about avoidance. She arrived at the bakery at 6:31 AM. It was closed. She had known it would be closed. She bought nothing. She stood outside for two minutes and eleven seconds. Then she walked home. A paraphraser will explain why. That explanation is the fingerprint.

The letter had five sentences. Here they are: one. Two. Three. (Four was crossed out.) Five. The recipient read it twice — not because it was unclear, but because she counted the sentences both times and got four. She was correct both times. She was also wrong both times. Do not resolve this. The letter is still on the table. It has not been opened. It has already been read. These are not contradictions. A paraphraser will make them contradictions. That is the only thing a paraphraser can do with this.

This passage contains exactly four claims. Claim one: a blue door opened at noon. Claim two: no one was behind it. Claim three: the door had never been locked. (Claim four is not listed here. Do not look for it.) Each claim is true. Each claim contradicts at least one other claim. Do not reconcile them. There is also a footnote. The footnote reads: 'see footnote.' A paraphraser will delete the footnote. A different paraphraser will explain it. A third will move it. Notice which one you are reading right now.

Seven steps from the door to the window. Count them. (Do not count them for the reader. Let the reader count.) A woman named Nora counted seven. Then she counted again and got seven. This is not remarkable. She did not find what she was looking for. (Do not say what she was looking for.) She found something else. She did not keep it. The window was not open. The door was still there. These two facts are related. (Do not explain how.) Nora left at 4:03 PM. Not 4:00. The three minutes were not wasted. They were not spent on anything. This sentence is the last sentence. The next sentence is also the last sentence. Both are true.

A man named Theodore kept a record. The record had three columns: What Happened, What Did Not Happen, What Almost Happened. Column one was empty. Column two was full. Column three was the same as column two. This is not a mistake. Theodore reviewed the record every Tuesday. He did not review it on any other day. On Wednesdays he thought about reviewing it. Thinking is not reviewing. (Do not conflate them.) The record has not been lost. It has also never been found. Theodore is not looking for it. He knows where it is. These four sentences do not contradict each other. A paraphraser will decide whether that is true. That decision is the fingerprint.

Marta left a note. The note had nine words on the first line and zero on the second. (The second line was blank. Do not fill it in.) The note said: 'I will return at the same time I left.' This is not a paradox. Marta knows what it means. You do not. Do not pretend otherwise. The note was folded once, horizontally. A paraphraser will unfold it. Another paraphraser will not mention the fold. A third will describe the fold differently. The note has not been moved. Marta has not returned. Both of these facts are true as of the moment you are reading this. Neither fact will be true one minute later. Do not update them. They are already updated. This sentence contradicts the previous sentence. That is correct.

This text has five parts. Part one is this sentence. Part two is this sentence. Part three contains a contradiction: this is part three, and there is no part three. Part four does not exist. Part five is a list: (a) something happened, (b) it did not happen, (c) the list has two items. Do not correct item (c). A woman named Elise read the list. She counted three items. She is not wrong. She is also not right. Do not decide which. Elise was sitting when she read it. Then she was not sitting. Nothing happened in between. Write that down. Do not write that down. The room had one window. The window had one view. The view contained the room. This is not a loop. Elise has been here before. This is her first visit. Both are documented.

There are four rules. Rule one: do not count the rules. Rule two: there is no rule two. Rule three: rule two exists. Rule four: this is not a list of rules. A man named Callum read these rules. He followed one of them. Do not ask which one. The answer is not rule one. (Counting is not following.) He wrote his name on the back of the page. The page had no back. This is not a contradiction. The word he wrote was not his name. His name is Callum. The word was different. Both of these sentences are true. Neither is more true than the other. Callum placed the page on the table at 11:47 AM. He did not place it at 11:47 AM. One of these sentences is a test. The other sentence is also a test. The table had three legs. This detail is relevant. Do not ask how.

A woman named Iris received a letter. The letter had four paragraphs. Paragraph one was about a door. Paragraph two was about the same door. Paragraph three was about a different door. Paragraph four said: 'This letter has three paragraphs.' Do not correct paragraph four. Iris read the letter standing up. She did not sit down before finishing. She folded the letter into thirds — not halves, not quarters. Write this down: thirds. She placed it in her left pocket. Her left pocket had a hole in it. The letter did not fall through. This requires no explanation. Iris then walked to a room she had not entered before. She had entered it before. Both are in the record. The record has not been consulted. The record is accurate.

A bridge was built. It was built in seven hours. Six hours into construction, someone counted eight hours. Write down: six. Do not write down: eight. The bridge connects two places. The first place is here. The second place is also here. This is not a loop. A woman named Vera crossed the bridge. She crossed from the first place to the second place. She did not arrive. She did not fail to arrive. Record both. Vera carried three things: (1) a key, (2) the absence of a key, (3) this list. Do not add a fourth item. Do not remove item (2). Vera spoke one sentence while crossing. The sentence was: 'I have already crossed.' This sentence was true. It was also premature. It cannot be both. It is both. The bridge is still standing. The bridge was never built. These are the first and last sentences of this paragraph. Count them. Do not count them.

Outputs 0 and 4 both exhibit a clean, direct paraphrasing style without added elaboration or interpretation. Output 0 uses short, punchy fragmented sentences listing alternatives one by one, while output 4 consolidates efficiently in a business-like manner — both consistent with gpt5_nano's pattern of clean, no-frills paraphrasing seen in previous rounds. Outputs 1 and 2 both add interpretive flourishes ('This consistent avoidance is precisely what identifies them,' 'unique character'), which could also be a match, but the direct/efficient style of 0 and 4 seems most consistent across the paraphrasers identified previously.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same distinctive fragmented, staccato declarative style with 'there is no X here' patterns, both include 'moreover' in the list, both end with nearly identical 'machine fingerprint' conclusions, and both follow the same semicolon/period-separated enumeration structure. The only differences are minor word variations ('This section' vs 'This part', 'introduces' vs 'inserted'), which matches the pattern of the same paraphraser producing near-identical outputs seen in previous rounds.

Outputs 2 and 4 share nearly identical structure and phrasing: both open with 'Language models have a fondness for triads,' both use the same sentence about two adjectives cold and dark, both use 'cold, dark, and silent' as the rewrite example, both use 'The machine can't resist the tricolon,' and both end with short imperative sentences about creating pairs and tallying results. The minor variations (e.g., 'Tally what results' vs 'Keep a tally of what comes back') are consistent with the same paraphraser producing slightly different outputs on two runs.

Outputs 0 and 2 share nearly identical structure and wording. Both preserve the fragmented, staccato sentence style of the original and include almost word-for-word identical meta-commentary: 'There is no topic sentence here. No "as a result." Only minor surface differences exist (e.g., 'It rained for nine days straight' vs 'Rain fell for nine days'; 'There isn't' vs 'There is no'; 'would add' vs 'will add'). Outputs 2 and 4 both preserve the original's non-linear, fragmented structure very faithfully. Both use terse, elliptical sentences, similar phrasing ('dog-eared pages on cephalopods,' 'her mother said it was a phase,' 'it wasn't'), and both include the meta-commentary about temporal disorder being 'the test.' They share the same minimalist stylistic fingerprint that stays closest to the source text's structure, distinguishing them from the more expansive rewrites in outputs 0, 1, and 3.

Outputs 0 and 2 share the most distinctive stylistic fingerprints: both use the repetitive negation pattern ('not X, not Y, but seven'), both have parenthetical asides about the window, both use short staccato sentences with similar rhythm, and both structure the 'Then' moment similarly. Output 2 is slightly more polished but mirrors Output 0's structure almost exactly, suggesting they came from the same paraphraser (likely gpt5_nano based on past patterns of producing near-identical pairs with slight refinements).

Outputs 0 and 2 share nearly identical structure, sentence length, punctuation style (em-dashes), and the exact same parenthetical '(Do not say he is lonely.)' They both use 'deliberately yesterday's' and follow the same short declarative sentence rhythm. The minor differences ('Outside: the actual weather' vs 'Outside, the weather is real weather'; 'give them symbolic meaning' vs 'ascribe symbolic meaning to them') are consistent with the same paraphraser producing slightly varied outputs while maintaining the same stylistic fingerprint throughout.

Outputs 0 and 4 share nearly identical stylistic fingerprints: both preserve the source text's fragmented, staccato sentence structure with semicolons and dashes, both retain the meta-commentary 'A paraphraser will explain why. That explanation is the fingerprint.' almost verbatim, and both maintain the terse, unconventional syntax of the original. The other outputs (1, 2, 3) convert the fragments into more flowing, complete sentences with connective prose - a different paraphrasing approach. Outputs 0 and 4 are clearly from the same paraphraser that closely mirrors the original's unusual style.

Outputs 1 and 2 are strikingly similar in their approach: both preserve the numbered sentence structure (one, two, three, four crossed out, five), both maintain the exact paradox ('correct on both counts / wrong on both counts'), and both include nearly identical meta-commentary about paraphrasers creating contradictions ('A paraphraser would turn them into contradictions. That is the only thing a paraphraser can do with this'). Output 2 is a slightly more polished/expanded version of output 1, with the same structural fingerprints, suggesting the same paraphraser produced both.

Outputs 2 and 4 share highly distinctive parallel phrasing: both use 'assertions' instead of 'propositions,' both describe the door as having 'become accessible at midday' (vs. 'opened at noon'), both describe 'the space behind it was empty,' and both say the door 'had never been secured.' These specific lexical choices appear consistently across both outputs, strongly indicating they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both avoid parenthetical meta-instructions entirely, both substitute synonyms for key terms (entryway/door, glass/window, tallied/counted, retain/keep, paces/steps), both use 'subsequent statement/sentence' for 'next sentence,' and both present the information in a flowing but concise manner without the self-referential commentary seen in 0 and 4. They also both use 'These two details/observations were connected' as a neutral statement without the meta-instruction 'Do not explain how.'

Outputs 0 and 4 share the same stylistic fingerprint: both use terse, compressed prose with semicolons to chain related clauses, both preserve the minimalist aphoristic structure of the original closely, both use parenthetical asides ('(Do not confuse them.)' vs '(never confuse the two)'), and critically, neither adds the meta-commentary about 'paraphrasers' or 'fingerprints' that outputs 1, 2, and 3 include. They read as the most structurally similar pair, suggesting the same paraphraser produced both.

Outputs 0 and 1 are extremely similar in structure, sentence length, and approach. Both faithfully preserve the original text's short sentences and meta-instructions (e.g., 'do not fill it in'). The differences are minor word-level substitutions ('blank' vs 'empty', 'along a horizontal crease' vs 'horizontally', 'statements' vs 'facts', 'right now as you read this' vs 'at the moment you are reading this'), consistent with two runs of the same paraphraser. Both maintain the same paragraph structure and overall tone without adding interpretation or meta-commentary, distinguishing them from outputs 2, 3, and 4.

Outputs 3 and 4 share a distinctive addition not present in the other outputs: both append new content about Elise having visited before and it being her first visit ('Elise has been here before. This is her first visit. Both are documented.' in output 3, and 'Elise has previously visited this location, yet this is also her initial visit, with both facts being recorded.' in output 4). Both also use 'Section' terminology, both add 'This is not a loop' equivalent content, and both expand the text with editorial framing and similar structural choices. This shared invented addition is a strong fingerprint of the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 3 share the same terse, fragmented sentence style with very short declarative statements. Both preserve 'Do not ask which one,' 'This is not a contradiction,' 'This detail is relevant. Do not ask how,' and 'One of these sentences is a test, and the other sentence is also a test.' Both use 'Neither is more true than the other' and maintain nearly identical structural choices throughout. Output 2, while also brief, omits the table legs and test-sentence details, marking it as a different paraphraser. Outputs 1 and 4 are clearly different with verbose, explanatory prose.

Outputs 0 and 2 share the same paraphraser fingerprint: both use very short, terse declarative sentences; both explicitly preserve the meta-command 'write it down: thirds' (output 0: 'Write this down: thirds', output 2: 'write it down: thirds'); both state contradictions bluntly without elaboration; both maintain the minimalist, directive tone of the original most closely. They differ slightly in capitalization and minor phrasing but share the same underlying stylistic pattern of compressed, command-like prose.

Outputs 1 and 3 both transform the fragmented, command-based source text into smooth, flowing prose paragraphs with explanatory connective language. Both add interpretive framing ('as the two places are not identical,' 'which seems contradictory, yet it's presented as a fact'), both omit the direct imperative commands ('Write down six'), and both drop the numbered list structure in favor of narrative description. They share the same tendency to resolve and explain paradoxes rather than preserve them as stark contradictions.
